---
title: "Chapter 09 — 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 08 · 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning](ch08.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [10 · 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning →](ch10.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Most of machine learning assumes someone has already labeled the data. Clustering throws that assumption away: you have points, no labels, and you want the algorithm to discover the natural groupings on its own. It is the workhorse of **unsupervised learning** — the branch of ML that finds structure in unlabeled data — and it shows up everywhere from customer segmentation to image compression to spotting fraud.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical ML, unsupervised side · used for segmentation, anomaly detection, and compression · the one key idea — group points so that within-group similarity is high and between-group similarity is low, with no labels to guide you.
:::

## 9.1 — What "no labels" really means

In supervised learning you minimize the gap between a prediction and a known answer. There is no answer key here. So the first question becomes: what does it even mean for a clustering to be "good"?

The honest answer is that goodness is defined by a **similarity** (or distance) measure you choose, plus an objective that rewards tight, well-separated groups. Change the distance and you change the clusters. With Euclidean distance, "similar" means "close in space." With cosine distance, it means "pointing the same direction" regardless of magnitude — which is why text and embeddings often use cosine.

Because there is no ground truth, clustering is **exploratory**: it proposes a structure, and a human decides whether that structure is meaningful. Two analysts running the same algorithm with different distance metrics or a different number of groups will both get "valid" answers. That is a feature, not a bug — but it means you must always sanity-check clusters against domain knowledge.

Here is the picture clustering is trying to recover — three blobs that a good algorithm should separate, plus a stray point that belongs to none. The blobs are drawn in one neutral color on purpose: in an unsupervised setting nothing is pre-labeled, so the color carries no information — it is just ink:

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <g fill="#4f86c6">
    <circle cx="55" cy="60" r="5"/><circle cx="70" cy="48" r="5"/><circle cx="45" cy="80" r="5"/><circle cx="65" cy="72" r="5"/><circle cx="80" cy="62" r="5"/>
    <circle cx="200" cy="50" r="5"/><circle cx="220" cy="40" r="5"/><circle cx="210" cy="65" r="5"/><circle cx="235" cy="58" r="5"/><circle cx="225" cy="72" r="5"/>
    <circle cx="150" cy="150" r="5"/><circle cx="170" cy="140" r="5"/><circle cx="135" cy="138" r="5"/><circle cx="160" cy="165" r="5"/><circle cx="180" cy="158" r="5"/>
  </g>
  <circle cx="278" cy="118" r="5" fill="#bbb" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="244" y="112" font-size="10" fill="#555">noise</text>
</svg>

## 9.2 — k-means: the prototypical partitioner

The intuition behind **k-means** is almost too simple: pick $k$ centers, assign every point to its nearest center, then move each center to the average of its assigned points, and repeat until nothing moves. Each cluster ends up represented by a single prototype — its **centroid** — and a point belongs to whichever centroid it is closest to.

Formally, k-means minimizes the **within-cluster sum of squares** (also called inertia):

$$ J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2 $$

where $C_i$ is the set of points in cluster $i$ and $\mu_i$ is its centroid. The algorithm is **Lloyd's algorithm**, and it alternates two steps that each can only lower $J$:

- **Assign:** put each point in the cluster of its nearest centroid.
- **Update:** recompute each centroid as the mean of its members.

```python
import numpy as np

def kmeans(X, k, iters=100, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    # init: pick k distinct points as centers (deterministic given seed)
    c = X[rng.choice(len(X), k, replace=False)]
    for _ in range(iters):
        # assign: nearest center for each point (n x k distances)
        d = ((X[:, None] - c[None]) ** 2).sum(2)
        labels = d.argmin(1)
        # update: mean of each cluster; keep old center if empty
        new_c = np.array([X[labels == j].mean(0) if (labels == j).any()
                          else c[j] for j in range(k)])
        if np.allclose(new_c, c):    # converged
            break
        c = new_c
    return labels, c

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    true = np.array([[0., 0.], [5., 5.]])
    X = np.vstack([rng.normal(true[0], .5, (50, 2)),
                   rng.normal(true[1], .5, (50, 2))])
    _, c = kmeans(X, 2)
    # every true center must be matched by some found center (order-free)
    for t in true:
        assert min(np.linalg.norm(ci - t) for ci in c) < 0.5
    print("centers:\n", c)
```

k-means is fast and easy to reason about, which is why it is usually the first thing people try. Each iteration costs $O(nkd)$ — for $n$ points, $k$ centers, and $d$ dimensions, you compute one distance per point–center pair — so it is linear in every quantity that matters and scales to large data. But it carries baggage. It only recovers **convex, roughly spherical, equal-size** blobs, because "nearest centroid" carves space into straight-sided (Voronoi) regions. It also assumes the blobs have **similar variance/density**: a tight cluster sitting next to a diffuse one gets its outer members stolen by the diffuse one's centroid. And it fails on **anisotropic** (stretched, elongated) blobs, where the true groups are cigar-shaped but k-means insists on round cells. On top of the shape assumptions it is sensitive to **feature scale** — a feature measured in thousands will dominate one measured in fractions — so you almost always standardize first, and the squared term makes it sensitive to outliers, which yank centroids around.

::: {.callout-warning}
k-means converges to a *local* minimum, and the result depends entirely on where the centers start. A single random start can land you in a bad partition. Always run it several times with different seeds (scikit-learn's `n_init`) and keep the lowest inertia.
:::

## 9.3 — Initialization, choosing k, and k-means++

The two practical headaches with k-means are *where do the centers start* and *what number is $k$*.

**Initialization.** Naive random starts can place two centers inside the same true blob, leaving a real cluster unclaimed. **k-means++** fixes this by spreading the initial centers out: pick the first center at random, then pick each subsequent center with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest center already chosen. Points far from every existing center are likely to be picked, so the seeds tend to land in different blobs. It costs almost nothing and is the default in every serious implementation.

**Choosing k.** Since you do not know the true number of groups, you sweep over candidate values and look at how the clustering quality changes. Two standard tools:

- **The elbow method.** Plot inertia $J$ against $k$. Inertia always drops as $k$ grows (more centers fit tighter), but the drop slows once you have captured the real structure. The "elbow" — where the curve bends from steep to shallow — is a heuristic estimate of $k$.

<svg viewBox="0 0 240 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="35" y1="15" x2="35" y2="120" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="35" y1="120" x2="220" y2="120" stroke="#888" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polyline points="50,30 80,70 110,98 140,108 170,113 200,116" fill="none" stroke="#4f86c6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="98" r="6" fill="none" stroke="#e07b53" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="118" y="92" font-size="10" fill="#e07b53">elbow (k=3)</text>
  <text x="120" y="138" font-size="10" fill="#555">k →</text>
  <text x="8" y="70" font-size="10" fill="#555" transform="rotate(-90 12,70)">inertia</text>
</svg>

- **The silhouette score.** For each point, compare $a$ = its mean distance to the *other points in its own cluster* against $b$ = its mean distance to all points in the *nearest other* cluster. The silhouette is

$$ s = \frac{b - a}{\max(a, b)} \in [-1, 1] $$

A value near 1 means the point sits comfortably in its cluster; near 0 means it is on a boundary; negative means it was probably assigned to the wrong cluster. Average $s$ over all points and pick the $k$ that maximizes it.

::: {.callout-tip}
The elbow is often ambiguous — real data rarely bends sharply. When the elbow is unclear, trust the silhouette score, and above all trust whether the resulting clusters mean something to a domain expert. The "right" $k$ is the one that is useful, not the one a metric blesses by a hair.
:::

## 9.4 — Hierarchical clustering and dendrograms

What if you do not want to commit to a single $k$ at all? **Hierarchical clustering** builds a whole tree of nested groupings, from "every point is its own cluster" up to "everything is one cluster," and lets you slice it at any level afterward.

The common bottom-up variant is **agglomerative clustering**. Start with each point as its own cluster, then repeatedly merge the two closest clusters until only one remains. The recipe for "closest" is the **linkage**, and it changes the shape of the result dramatically:

| Linkage | Distance between clusters | Tendency |
|---|---|---|
| Single | Closest pair of points | Long, chaining clusters; sensitive to noise |
| Complete | Farthest pair of points | Compact, roughly equal-diameter clusters |
| Average | Mean over all cross pairs | A balanced compromise |
| Ward | Increase in within-cluster variance | Tight, similar-size clusters (k-means-like) |

The merge history is drawn as a **dendrogram** — a tree where the height of each merge equals the distance at which two clusters joined. Cutting the dendrogram with a horizontal line at some height gives you a flat clustering; cut low for many small clusters, high for a few big ones. The vertical gaps between merges hint at natural groupings: a tall gap means the next merge bridges two genuinely distant groups.

The diagram below shows the nesting structure agglomerative clustering produces: the leaves at the bottom are individual points, and as you move up they merge into bigger and bigger clusters until everything joins at the root.

```{mermaid}
flowchart BT
    A["A"] --> AB["{A,B}"]
    B["B"] --> AB
    C["C"] --> CD["{C,D}"]
    D["D"] --> CD
    AB --> ABCD["{A,B,C,D}"]
    CD --> ABCD
```

The upside is that you get the full hierarchy and a visual you can reason about, with no preset $k$. The downside is cost: a naive implementation is $O(n^3)$ in time and $O(n^2)$ in memory, so plain agglomerative clustering does not scale to very large datasets without approximation.

## 9.5 — DBSCAN: density, arbitrary shapes, and noise

k-means and Ward both assume blobs. But what if your clusters are crescents, rings, or long filaments — and what if some points are just junk that belongs to no cluster at all? **DBSCAN** (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) handles exactly these cases by defining clusters as **dense regions separated by sparse ones**, rather than by distance to a center.

DBSCAN has two parameters: a radius $\varepsilon$ (how close counts as "neighboring") and `minPts` (how many neighbors make a region "dense"). It then labels every point as one of three types:

- **Core point:** has at least `minPts` points within distance $\varepsilon$ — it sits in a dense region.
- **Border point:** within $\varepsilon$ of a core point but not dense itself — the edge of a cluster.
- **Noise point:** neither — an outlier that joins no cluster.

A cluster grows by starting at a core point and absorbing everything **density-reachable** from it: walk from core point to core point through overlapping $\varepsilon$-neighborhoods, picking up border points along the way. Because clusters follow density rather than a centroid, they can take any shape, and DBSCAN figures out the number of clusters by itself.

The classic illustration is two interleaved crescents ("two moons"). k-means, forced to draw a straight boundary between two centroids, slices both crescents in half; DBSCAN follows the density and recovers each crescent whole:

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <text x="10" y="14" font-size="11" fill="#555">k-means (straight cut)</text>
  <g fill="#4f86c6">
    <circle cx="30" cy="70" r="3"/><circle cx="45" cy="55" r="3"/><circle cx="62" cy="48" r="3"/><circle cx="80" cy="46" r="3"/><circle cx="98" cy="50" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="55" cy="95" r="3"/><circle cx="72" cy="85" r="3"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="#e07b53">
    <circle cx="98" cy="78" r="3"/><circle cx="115" cy="70" r="3"/><circle cx="130" cy="68" r="3"/><circle cx="146" cy="74" r="3"/><circle cx="158" cy="88" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="116" cy="92" r="3"/><circle cx="133" cy="90" r="3"/>
  </g>
  <line x1="100" y1="30" x2="100" y2="120" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <text x="210" y="14" font-size="11" fill="#555">DBSCAN (follows density)</text>
  <g fill="#4f86c6">
    <circle cx="220" cy="70" r="3"/><circle cx="235" cy="55" r="3"/><circle cx="252" cy="48" r="3"/><circle cx="270" cy="46" r="3"/><circle cx="288" cy="50" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="245" cy="95" r="3"/><circle cx="262" cy="85" r="3"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="#5aa469">
    <circle cx="288" cy="78" r="3"/><circle cx="305" cy="70" r="3"/><circle cx="320" cy="68" r="3"/><circle cx="336" cy="74" r="3"/><circle cx="348" cy="88" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="306" cy="92" r="3"/><circle cx="323" cy="90" r="3"/>
  </g>
  <circle cx="300" cy="130" r="3" fill="#bbb" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="308" y="134" font-size="9" fill="#555">noise</text>
</svg>

The two big payoffs are that you do **not** pick $k$ in advance, and that **noise is a first-class outcome** — making DBSCAN a natural anomaly detector. The cost is parameter sensitivity: choose $\varepsilon$ too small and everything looks like noise; too large and separate clusters fuse into one. DBSCAN also struggles when clusters have very different densities, since a single global $\varepsilon$ cannot be tight enough for the dense cluster and loose enough for the sparse one at the same time (the **HDBSCAN** variant addresses this by working across scales).

::: {.callout-warning}
DBSCAN's `minPts` and $\varepsilon$ are not interchangeable knobs you can set blindly. A common practice is to fix `minPts` near twice the data's dimensionality, then read a good $\varepsilon$ off the "knee" of a sorted k-nearest-neighbor distance plot. Guessing both at once usually gives one giant cluster or all noise.
:::

## 9.6 — Gaussian Mixture Models and the EM algorithm

k-means makes a hard call: a point is in exactly one cluster. But real boundaries are fuzzy — a customer might be 70% "budget shopper" and 30% "occasional splurger." **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)** capture this with **soft clustering**: each point gets a *probability* of belonging to each cluster.

A GMM assumes the data was generated by a mixture of $k$ Gaussian distributions, each with its own mean $\mu_i$, covariance $\Sigma_i$, and mixing weight $\pi_i$ (how prevalent that component is). The density of any point is the weighted sum:

$$ p(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \pi_i \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i) $$

Because each Gaussian has a full covariance matrix, components can be stretched and tilted ellipses, not just spheres — a strict generalization of k-means.

We fit a GMM with **Expectation–Maximization (EM)**, an iterative two-step dance that mirrors Lloyd's algorithm but with probabilities. In the **E-step** we compute, for every point, its **responsibility** $\gamma_{ij}$ — the posterior probability that component $i$ generated point $j$ — holding the parameters fixed. In the **M-step** we hold those responsibilities fixed and re-estimate each component's weight, mean, and covariance as responsibility-weighted statistics of the data. Each round is guaranteed not to decrease the data likelihood, and we repeat until it stops climbing:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    init["init params<br/>(often via k-means)"] --> E["E-step:<br/>responsibilities γ"]
    E --> M["M-step:<br/>reweight μ, Σ, π"]
    M --> conv{"likelihood<br/>converged?"}
    conv -- no --> E
    conv -- yes --> done["done"]
```

The responsibility and M-step updates are short enough to write out directly:

```python
import numpy as np

def em_step(X, mu, Sig, pi):
    n, k = len(X), len(pi)
    # E-step: responsibility of component i for point j
    g = np.zeros((n, k))
    for i in range(k):
        d = X - mu[i]
        inv = np.linalg.inv(Sig[i])
        # unnormalized Gaussian density * mixing weight
        e = np.exp(-0.5 * np.einsum('nj,jl,nl->n', d, inv, d))
        g[:, i] = pi[i] * e / np.sqrt(np.linalg.det(Sig[i]))
    g /= g.sum(1, keepdims=True)          # normalize over components
    # M-step: responsibility-weighted re-estimates
    Nk = g.sum(0)
    pi = Nk / n
    mu = (g.T @ X) / Nk[:, None]
    Sig = np.array([(g[:, i, None] * (X - mu[i])).T @ (X - mu[i]) / Nk[i]
                    for i in range(k)])
    return mu, Sig, pi, g

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    X = np.vstack([rng.normal(0, .4, (60, 2)), rng.normal(4, .4, (60, 2))])
    mu = np.array([[ -1., 0.], [3., 1.]])           # rough init
    Sig = np.array([np.eye(2), np.eye(2)]); pi = np.array([.5, .5])
    for _ in range(30):
        mu, Sig, pi, _ = em_step(X, mu, Sig, pi)
    # the two means must separate toward 0 and 4
    assert min(np.linalg.norm(m) for m in mu) < 1.0
    assert max(np.linalg.norm(m - 4) for m in mu) < 1.5
    print("means:\n", mu.round(2))
```

In practice the most important knob is `covariance_type`, which constrains the shape each $\Sigma_i$ may take: `full` (any tilted ellipse), `tied` (all components share one covariance), `diag` (axis-aligned ellipses), and `spherical` (round). The `spherical` case with equal weights and hardened assignments is exactly k-means — so k-means is the rigid special case sitting at one end of the GMM family. Like k-means, EM only finds a local optimum and depends on initialization (often k-means is run first to seed it). The soft assignments are the real prize: they tell you *how confident* the model is about each point, which is exactly what you want at cluster boundaries or for downstream probabilistic reasoning.

## 9.7 — Spectral clustering, briefly

Some data is grouped by **connectivity** rather than compactness — think two interlocking spirals, where points in the same spiral are close to their neighbors but the spiral as a whole is far from spherical. Centroid methods fail here. **Spectral clustering** sidesteps the problem by clustering a *graph* instead of the raw points.

The idea: build a similarity graph (connect each point to its nearest neighbors, with edge weights $W_{ij}$), form the **graph Laplacian** from it, and take the eigenvectors corresponding to its smallest non-trivial eigenvalues. The Laplacian is

$$ L = D - W $$

where $W$ is the weighted adjacency matrix and $D$ is the diagonal **degree** matrix with $D_{ii} = \sum_j W_{ij}$ (each node's total edge weight). Those bottom eigenvectors give each point a new, low-dimensional coordinate in which the tangled clusters become simple blobs — at which point you run plain k-means. In effect, the eigen-decomposition unfolds the manifold so that connectivity turns into proximity. It is powerful for non-convex shapes and graph-structured data, but the eigen-decomposition is costly on large datasets, and you still must choose how to build the graph and how many clusters to extract.

## 9.8 — Choosing an algorithm, judging clusters, and putting them to work

With five families on the table, the practical question is *which one when*. The table below lines them up on the axes that usually decide it:

| Algorithm | Need to preset $k$? | Cluster shape | Scales to large $n$? | Handles outliers? | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| k-means | yes | convex, spherical | yes ($O(nkd)$/iter) | no | hard |
| Hierarchical | no (cut later) | flexible via linkage | no ($O(n^3)$) | no | hard |
| DBSCAN | no | arbitrary | yes (with index) | yes (noise label) | hard |
| GMM (EM) | yes | ellipsoidal | moderate | no | soft |
| Spectral | yes | arbitrary / connectivity | no (eigendecomp) | no | hard |

A rough reading: reach for **k-means** when you expect round, comparable blobs and want speed; **DBSCAN** when shapes are weird and you need outliers flagged; **GMM** when you want soft, probabilistic membership; **hierarchical** when you want to inspect structure at every granularity; and **spectral** when groups are defined by connectivity rather than compactness.

Once you have clusters, how do you know they are any good? Evaluation splits into two camps depending on whether you have any external truth.

**Internal metrics** use only the data and the clustering itself — no labels. They reward tight, well-separated groups:

- **Silhouette score** (from 9.3): high when points are close to their own cluster and far from others.
- **Davies–Bouldin index:** average ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster separation; lower is better.
- **Calinski–Harabasz index:** ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster dispersion; higher is better.

**External metrics** compare your clusters against known labels, when you happen to have them (often only on a benchmark or a hand-labeled sample). They measure agreement while ignoring the fact that cluster *names* are arbitrary:

- **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI):** agreement on which pairs of points are grouped together, corrected for chance.
- **Normalized Mutual Information (NMI):** how much knowing the cluster tells you about the true label.

Why bother clustering at all? Three recurring uses anchor the field. **Segmentation** groups customers, documents, or pixels so each group can be treated differently — the bread and butter of marketing analytics. **Anomaly detection** flags points that fit no cluster (DBSCAN noise) or sit far from every centroid as candidates for fraud, defects, or intrusions. And **compression** replaces each data point with its cluster prototype — **vector quantization**. Image color quantization is the textbook case and it is *literally k-means run on pixel RGB values*: treat each pixel as a point in 3-D color space, cluster into 16 centroids, and store only a 4-bit cluster index per pixel plus the 16-color palette. The prototype *is* the centroid, so compression and §9.2 are the same algorithm wearing different hats.

## 9.x — Key takeaways

- Clustering is unsupervised: there are no labels, so "good" is defined by your chosen distance metric and objective, and must be validated against domain sense.
- **k-means** is fast ($O(nkd)$ per iteration) but assumes spherical, equal-size, equal-variance blobs and fails on stretched or unequal-density ones; standardize features, use **k-means++**, and run multiple seeds.
- Pick $k$ with the elbow and silhouette heuristics — but treat them as suggestions, not verdicts; silhouette uses $a$ = mean distance to other points in the same cluster vs $b$ = mean distance to the nearest other cluster.
- **Hierarchical clustering** builds a dendrogram you can cut at any level; the **linkage** rule controls cluster shape, and it does not scale cheaply.
- **DBSCAN** finds arbitrary shapes, needs no preset $k$, and labels outliers as noise — at the price of sensitivity to $\varepsilon$ and `minPts`.
- **GMMs** with **EM** give soft, probabilistic assignments and can model tilted, unequal ellipses; the `covariance_type` knob controls shape, and the spherical, equal-weight, hardened case *is* k-means.
- **Spectral clustering** handles connectivity-based, non-convex clusters by clustering the eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian $L = D - W$.
- Use the algorithm-comparison table to pick a method, then evaluate with **internal** metrics (silhouette, Davies–Bouldin) when you have no labels and **external** metrics (ARI, NMI) when you do.

## 9.y — See also

- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering:** scaling and distance choices that make or break clustering.
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning:** PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP, which often precede or visualize clustering.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection:** the broader evaluation toolkit behind the metrics here.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning:** the probabilistic foundations under GMMs and EM.
- **Chapter 37 — Anomaly Detection:** the dedicated treatment of the outlier-finding use case touched on here.
- **Chapter 35 — Recommender Systems:** where segmentation and vector quantization reappear in practice.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 08 · 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning](ch08.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [10 · 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning →](ch10.html)
